title: Hypervigilance at Home: Turning the Threat Dial Down
series_subtitle: A Field Guide for Frontline Folks and Their Families
cover_tagline: Your house is not a scene. Teach your body the difference.

sections.heading: Why Home Can Feel Like a Threat Zone

content.text: For many frontline folks, hypervigilance does not switch off at the front door. Your body keeps scanning for danger even when your mind knows you are home.
That mismatch creates irritability, overreactions, and emotional distance. Family members often read it as rejection when it is actually an activated nervous system.

items: Approach from behind or surprise touch

content.text: Think of activation like a dial, not an on/off switch. Your goal is not zero activation. Your goal is to turn the dial down sooner and more often.

lines: Breathing is easier, tone is steady, attention is flexible.
Scanning rises, patience drops, body tension climbs.
Conversation becomes unsafe and connection drops fast.
Why this works: labeling state and orienting to safety cues reduces autonomic arousal and improves prefrontal control.

content.text: A predictable re-entry routine lowers conflict and protects connection. Build one protocol and run it daily.

items.text: tell your household when you are in decompression mode.
hydrate, breathe, shower, or move before full engagement.
eye contact, hug, and one direct check-in question.
call your state before your tone leaks onto others.

items: Use names before touching or approaching from behind.
Agree on one phrase that means 'I need to reset.'
Do not process major conflict in red zone.
Set a same-day repair rule after hard moments.

sections.heading: When Hypervigilance at Home Needs More Support

items: Family members feel afraid or constantly on eggshells.
You avoid home spaces to avoid activation.
Activation leads to frequent shouting, shutdown, or substance use.
Relationship repair attempts are not reducing recurrence.

content.text: Track one home trigger and one successful reset each day. Your weekly metric is fewer red moments and quicker re-engagement.

worksheets.title: Worksheet: Hypervigilance at Home - Trigger Map
worksheets.intro: Use this to identify specific home triggers and your fastest dial-down actions.

question_groups.label: What home situations activated your threat response?
question_groups.hint: Include noise, transitions, family dynamics, and environmental factors.

question_groups.label: What did activation cost in the moment?
question_groups.hint: Connection, patience, sleep, tone, parenting consistency, or conflict fallout.

question_groups.label: What one reset action worked best?
question_groups.hint: Choose one action to repeat every day this week.

numbered_prompts.hint: What did you notice first in your body or tone?
What lowered your arousal in under two minutes?
If stress leaked onto others, what exact repair did you do?

worksheets.closing_note: A calmer home starts with predictable resets, not perfect days.

worksheets.title: Worksheet: Hypervigilance at Home - Family Re-Entry Plan
worksheets.intro: Use this with family to define your post-shift transition and reduce avoidable conflict.

question_groups.label: What is your decompression window?
question_groups.hint: Set exact duration and what you will do during it.

question_groups.label: What are the do/don'ts during that window?
question_groups.hint: Clarify approach, noise, requests, and communication boundaries.

question_groups.label: How will you reconnect after decompression?

numbered_prompts.hint: What phrase means decompression window is active?
What exact time/action marks re-entry to family mode?
What one change will improve this protocol next week?

worksheets.closing_note: Shared structure lowers stress for everyone in the house.

paragraphs: You are not failing at home because you do not care. You are carrying a threat system that stayed on too long.
With practice and shared agreements, your home can become a recovery space again.
closing.highlight: Your family does not need perfection. They need predictability and repair.
